title: The half-lap joint
subtitle: Marking, cutting, and fitting a half-lap joint in seasoned hardwood
excerpt: A half-lap joint connects two pieces of the same thickness by reducing each by half at the crossing point, so they sit flush when assembled. It is the standard joint for picture frames, simple furniture frames, and corner brackets. This technique covers marking out with a knife, sawing the shoulder, chiselling the waste, and fitting the joint.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for half-lap joint proportions; Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for chisel approach to lap joint waste removal.

glossaryTerms.definition: Timber stabilised at 8 to 12 percent moisture content. Half-lap joints in seasoned wood remain tight as the pieces are at equilibrium with the room environment.
A wood joint where each of two pieces is reduced by half its thickness at the joint area, so the two pieces overlap and sit flush when assembled.
The slot left by a saw cut. The kerf from the shoulder cut in a half-lap defines the exact position of the joint face.

content.text: The
half-lap joint

is one of the most useful hand-cut joints: two pieces of equal thickness that intersect, each reduced by half so the combined thickness at the crossing equals the thickness of either piece alone. The joint provides full-face gluing area and, when fitted correctly, requires no fasteners. Using
seasoned wood
is important; wood at equilibrium moisture does not swell after the joint is glued, which would open the shoulder line and show a gap.
Marking out
Accurate marking is the whole of the half-lap joint. The
kerf
from the shoulder saw cut is the datum for everything else. Mark with a marking knife, not a pencil; knife lines are thinner and leave a step in the wood surface that guides the saw.

Place the two pieces next to each other and mark the width of one across the face of the other at the joint position. This is the width of the lap; it must equal the exact width of the mating piece. Score this line with the marking knife, then run the knife along a square to carry the line across both faces and both edges.

Set a marking gauge to exactly half the piece thickness. Run the gauge from both faces to mark the half-depth line on both edges of each piece, within the lap area. The gauge lines must meet; if they do not, the gauge setting is not exactly half-thickness. Adjust and re-mark.

Mark the waste area (the half that will be removed) with diagonal pencil lines to avoid confusion during chiselling. A half-lap chiselled from the wrong face produces a joint that stands proud rather than sitting flush.
Cutting

Saw the shoulder cut on the waste side of the knife line; the saw kerf must not remove any of the finished joint face. Use a tenon saw and cut just to the half-depth gauge line on each edge. Check the saw cut depth on both edges before proceeding.

Chisel out the waste with the 25 mm firmer chisel. Work from the edge inward in 3 mm passes; do not try to remove the full depth in one pass. Begin with the chisel bevel down, 5 mm back from the shoulder line, driving downward with the mallet. Take a horizontal slice at the half-depth mark to remove the step. Repeat working toward the shoulder line.

Final-fit the lap floor with the 12 mm bevel-edge chisel held flat, bevel up, paring across the grain in light hand-driven strokes. The floor must be flat; test with a straight edge or the back of the chisel. The shoulder line must be exactly at the knife line; test with a square. A gap at the shoulder shows as a hairline when the joint is assembled; correct by paring the shoulder face back 0.5 mm.
Testing the fit

A correctly fitted half-lap joint slides together with hand pressure and sits flush on both faces at the crossing. The joint should not require mallet force to close; a joint that needs driving has a low spot in the floor that concentrates the interference. Find the high spot (a smear of pencil on the mating face will transfer to the high point) and pare it down. The joint should be snug but removable without wedging.
Common mistakes
Sawing on the line rather than the waste side: the saw removes the kerf width from the joint face. Always saw with the kerf entirely in the waste.
Gauge not at exactly half-thickness: one piece will sit proud of the other. Re-set the gauge and re-mark; the pencil diagonal marks make it clear which piece needs correction.
Chiselling across the grain for the final floor pass: cross-grain chisel strokes in hardwood tear the surface. Pare parallel to the grain on the final pass.
